answer.rationale: Pan-canonical lexical demonstration. John 1:14 ὁ λόγος σὰρξ ἐγένετο: sarx G4561 (flesh, human nature) as predicate of egeneto G1096 (became, aorist indicative). The eternal Logos became flesh, not merely appeared. 1 Tim 2:5 ἄνθρωπος Χριστὸς Ἰησοῦς: anthrōpos G444 (man, member of the human race) in apposition to Christ Jesus, the one mediator between God and humanity. Heb 2:14 paraplēsiōs meteschen G3898 + G3348 (he likewise shared) of haima G129 and sarx G4561 (blood and flesh) with the children. Heb 2:17 katā panta tois adelphois homoiōthēnai G3666 (made like his brothers in every respect). Heb 4:15 pepeirasmenon G3985 kata panta kath' homoiotēta G3665 chōris hamartias G266 (tested in every way as we are, yet without sin). Phil 2:7-8 morphēn doulou G3444 + G1401 + en homoiōmati anthrōpōn genomenos G3667 (form of a servant, in the likeness of men). Rom 1:3 ek spermatos Dauid kata sarka G4690 + G4561 (of the seed of David according to the flesh). Luke 2:52 proekopten G4298 imperfect (he kept advancing in wisdom and stature). 1 John 4:2-3 names denial of Christ come in flesh as the antichrist spirit, perfect participle elēlythota G2064 (has come and remains in the flesh).
answer.notes: Christ's full humanity is the only doctrine in Scripture that an apostolic letter explicitly names as the test of the antichrist spirit. 1 John 4:2-3 sets the lexical floor: every spirit that confesses Iēsoun Christon en sarki elēlythota (Jesus Christ come in flesh) is from God; every spirit that does not is not from God and is the spirit of antichrist. 2 John 7 repeats it. This is unique among the questions in the baseline: Scripture itself supplies the cult-marker verdict in its own vocabulary, not by inference. Heb 2:14-18 makes Christ's humanity soteriologically load-bearing: he had to be made like his brothers in every respect (homoiōthēnai G3666) in order to make propitiation (hilaskesthai G2433) for the sins of the people; without full humanity there is no priesthood, no mediation, no atonement, no resurrection of the body. 1 Tim 2:5 makes it Trinitarian-Christological boundary: the one mediator is anthrōpos Christos Iēsous, man Christ Jesus. Public-record carriers of denial: Docetism (1st-2nd century gnostic teaching that Christ only appeared human, the heresy 1 John explicitly anathematizes); Marcionism (Christ appeared in adult human form without true birth or flesh, refuted by Tertullian); Apollinarianism (Christ took a human body but had no rational human soul, condemned at Constantinople 381 and Chalcedon 451); Eutychianism / extreme monophysitism (the two natures merged into one new nature so that the humanity was absorbed into the divinity, condemned at Chalcedon 451); Christian Science under Mary Baker Eddy (matter is illusion, so Christ's flesh is not real material body); some popular preaching that functionally treats Jesus as a divine being merely wearing humanity as a costume drifts toward the same error. Fruit-vs-precision nuance: most lay Christians cannot articulate dyophysite Chalcedonian formula but functionally confess the incarnation through Christmas, the Lord's Supper, and the affirmation that Jesus is the Son of Man who suffered, died, and rose bodily. The bar is denial of true humanity, not failure to articulate the technical formula. Even the miaphysite Oriental Orthodox lineages (Coptic, Armenian, Syriac, Ethiopian, Eritrean, Indian) who reject the Chalcedonian formula confess Christ's full humanity unambiguously; their disagreement with Chalcedon is over how the natures relate, not whether the humanity is real and complete. The cult-marker line is therefore univocal across all tracked historic Christian lineages and is given by Scripture itself.

stem_audit.notes: Statement asserts the verdict ('Jesus Christ is fully and truly human, taking on a complete human nature including body and rational soul') but the assertion is the plain gloss of the lexical anchors: sarx egeneto (John 1:14), anthrōpos (1 Tim 2:5), haima + sarx meteschen (Heb 2:14), homoiōthēnai kata panta (Heb 2:17), en sarki elēlythota (1 John 4:2). The phrase 'body and rational soul' is Chalcedonian vocabulary, but the rational-soul clause is itself lexically grounded in Christ's growth in wisdom (Luke 2:52 proekopten sophia), his anguish of soul (John 12:27 hē psychē mou tetaraktai), and his commitment of spirit at death (Luke 23:46 pneuma). No confessional smuggling beyond the lexical evidence.

lay_summary.reasoning: Verdict: Jesus Christ is fully and truly human. He took on a complete human nature, with a real body of flesh and blood and a real human mind and soul. He was not a divine being merely pretending to be human, and he was not human in body only. The strongest reasons from Scripture are direct. John 1:14 says, 'the Word became flesh and dwelt among us.' First Timothy 2:5 calls him 'the man Christ Jesus.' Hebrews 2:14 says he 'shared in flesh and blood' the same way we do, because the children share in those things. Hebrews 2:17 says he had to be made like his brothers in every respect. Hebrews 4:15 says he was tested in every way as we are, yet without sin. Philippians 2:7 says he took the form of a servant and was made in the likeness of men. Romans 1:3 says he was descended from David according to the flesh. Luke 2:52 says he grew in wisdom and stature. He got hungry (Matthew 4:2), he got thirsty (John 19:28), he wept (John 11:35), he slept (Mark 4:38), he bled and died (John 19:34). First John 4:2 and 3 say that any spirit that does not confess Jesus has come in the flesh is not from God and is the spirit of antichrist. The strongest counterargument inside the Bible is that Scripture also affirms Jesus is fully God (John 1:1, John 20:28, Romans 9:5, Titus 2:13, Hebrews 1:8), and critics from ancient times have asked how a real human could also be God. Could God really hunger, thirst, suffer, and die. Some early teachers known as the docetists answered no, and said Jesus only appeared to be human. The Bible answers the question itself. John writes specifically to refute the docetists in 1 John, telling Christians that confessing Jesus Christ has come in the flesh is the test of a spirit being from God. Hebrews 2 and 4 make the humanity load-bearing for the gospel itself. Only a real human can stand in for real humans, only a real human can die for human sin, only a real high priest who has shared real human weakness can sympathize with us. The Bible does not say Jesus is half-God and half-man, or God hiding inside a human costume. It says he is fully one and fully the other in one person. The doctrine stands on the strongest possible canonical footing, and the cult-marker line is given by Scripture itself in 1 John 4:2 and 3.
lay_summary.denominational_landscape: Every major historic Christian lineage confesses the full humanity of Christ without exception, and this is one of the few doctrines where Scripture itself names denial as the spirit of antichrist. The early fathers refuted docetism in the first and second centuries. Ignatius of Antioch wrote to the Trallians and the Smyrnaeans around 110 AD insisting that Christ was 'truly born, and ate and drank, truly persecuted under Pontius Pilate, truly crucified, and truly died.' Irenaeus refuted gnostic docetism in Against Heresies. Tertullian refuted Marcion's claim of a phantom Christ. Athanasius wrote On the Incarnation defending Christ's true human nature. The Council of Constantinople in 381 condemned Apollinarianism, which had taught that Christ took a human body but had no human soul. The Council of Chalcedon in 451 produced the definitive statement that Christ is 'truly God and truly man, of a reasonable soul and body, consubstantial with us according to the manhood, in all things like unto us, without sin.' Catholic teaching follows Chalcedon, confessing Christ as true God and true man with a rational human soul and a true human body. The Lutheran Augsburg Confession Article III confesses Christ as 'true God and true man' with two natures inseparably joined in one Person. The Anglican 39 Articles Article II confesses two whole and perfect natures, Godhead and Manhood, joined in one Person. The Reformed confessions all affirm the same. The Methodist Articles of Religion Article II uses the same language as Article II of the 39 Articles. Anabaptists at Schleitheim and Dordrecht confess Christ's true incarnation. Pentecostals in the Assemblies of God Statement of Fundamental Truths confess Christ's virgin birth and sinless human life. Eastern Orthodoxy follows Chalcedon and the Trisagion. The Oriental Orthodox churches (Coptic, Armenian, Syriac, Ethiopian, Indian) reject the Chalcedonian formula but confess Christ's full humanity just as strongly. Their disagreement with Chalcedon is over how the two natures relate to each other, not over whether the humanity is real and complete. So even at the most contested point in church history, the bare doctrine of Christ's full humanity is held by every lineage that calls itself Christian. Public-record carriers of explicit denial place themselves outside historic Christianity. Docetism, condemned in the New Testament itself. Marcionism, condemned by the early church. Apollinarianism, condemned at Constantinople 381 (Christ took a human body but no human soul). Eutychianism, condemned at Chalcedon 451 (the human nature was absorbed into the divine, leaving one merged nature). Christian Science under Mary Baker Eddy, which teaches that matter is illusion and therefore Christ had no real physical body. Functional denial appears in some popular preaching that treats Jesus as God merely wearing humanity as a disguise, but that is a teaching error to correct, not a settled denomination position. Scripture itself, in 1 John 4:2 and 3, names denial of Christ come in the flesh as the spirit of antichrist.

scripture.force: sarx = flesh, human nature, material humanity. egeneto = became (aorist indicative active, completed event). 'The Word became flesh.' Predicate nominative construction asserting an essential transformation, the eternal Logos truly assumed human nature. The aorist marks the singular historical event of incarnation

scripture.key_term: ἄνθρωπος Χριστὸς Ἰησοῦς (anthrōpos Christos Iēsous) G444
scripture.force: anthrōpos = man, member of the human race. Nominative masculine singular in apposition to Christos Iēsous. The one mediator between God and humanity is named precisely as anthrōpos. Direct lexical assertion of Christ's humanity in the same breath as his mediatorial work

scripture.key_term: αἵματος καὶ σαρκός... παραπλησίως μετέσχεν (haimatos kai sarkos... paraplēsiōs meteschen) G129 + G4561 + G3898 + G3348
scripture.force: haima = blood, sarx = flesh, paraplēsiōs = in like manner / similarly, meteschen = shared (aorist of metechō G3348). Because the children share in flesh and blood, he himself likewise shared in the same things. The adverb paraplēsiōs G3898 is exact, asserting that Christ's participation in human nature is parallel to the children's, not derivative or symbolic

scripture.key_term: κατὰ πάντα τοῖς ἀδελφοῖς ὁμοιωθῆναι (kata panta tois adelphois homoiōthēnai) G3666
scripture.force: homoiōthēnai = to be made like (aorist passive infinitive of homoioō G3666). 'In every respect like his brothers'. Comprehensive identification with humanity is grounded in the soteriological purpose of his being a merciful and faithful high priest, hilaskesthai G2433 (to make propitiation) for the sins of the people. Without full humanity, no priesthood

scripture.key_term: πεπειρασμένον δὲ κατὰ πάντα καθ' ὁμοιότητα χωρὶς ἁμαρτίας (pepeirasmenon... kath' homoiotēta chōris hamartias) G3985 + G3665 + G266
scripture.force: pepeirasmenon = having been tested (perfect passive participle of peirazō G3985, completed action with continuing relevance). homoiotēs G3665 = likeness, resemblance. 'Tested in every respect according to our likeness, yet without sin.' Two clauses: full identification with human testing, no participation in human sinning

scripture.key_term: ἐν ὁμοιώματι ἀνθρώπων γενόμενος (en homoiōmati anthrōpōn genomenos) G3667 + G444 + G1096
scripture.force: homoiōma = likeness, form (the same noun used in Rom 8:3 for the likeness of sinful flesh). genomenos = having become (aorist middle participle). 'Being made in the likeness of men'. The phrase has been challenged by docetists, but Paul's next clause 'and being found in form as a man' (schēmati G4976 hōs anthrōpos) closes the door: he was truly a man

scripture.key_term: σχήματι εὑρεθεὶς ὡς ἄνθρωπος... ὑπήκοος μέχρι θανάτου (schēmati heuretheis hōs anthrōpos) G4976 + G444
scripture.force: schēma G4976 = outward appearance, fashion, observable form. heuretheis = having been found (aorist passive). 'Being found in observable form as a man, he humbled himself, becoming obedient to the point of death, even death on a cross.' Christ's humanity culminates in a real human death by crucifixion

scripture.key_term: ἐκ σπέρματος Δαυίδ κατὰ σάρκα (ek spermatos Dauid kata sarka) G4690 + G1138 + G4561
scripture.force: sperma G4690 = seed, offspring, physical descent. kata sarka = according to the flesh. Paul opens his most carefully structured letter by anchoring the Son physically in the line of David. Genealogical, biological descent from a human father. The phrase kata sarka is paired in v.4 with kata pneuma hagiōsynēs, the two-aspect Pauline Christological formula

scripture.key_term: προέκοπτεν σοφίᾳ καὶ ἡλικίᾳ (proekopten sophia kai hēlikia) G4298 + G4678 + G2244
scripture.force: proekopten = was advancing, was making progress (imperfect indicative active, continuous action). sophia = wisdom, hēlikia = age or stature. Jesus grew. The imperfect tense rules out a single moment of pretended growth. He actually progressed in wisdom over time, which entails a real human mind that learns. This grounds the Chalcedonian 'rational human soul' lexically

scripture.key_term: ἐδάκρυσεν ὁ Ἰησοῦς (edakrysen ho Iēsous) G1145
scripture.force: dakryō G1145 = to weep, shed tears. Aorist indicative active. The shortest verse in the Greek New Testament. Jesus wept at the tomb of Lazarus. The narrative names physical human grief expressed through tears, with the surrounding verses naming his being deeply moved in spirit and troubled (etaraxen heauton, v.33)

dipsaō G1372 = to thirst. Present indicative active. On the cross Jesus says 'I thirst', and the narrator notes this fulfills Scripture (Ps 69:21). Real physical bodily thirst at the point of death. The narrative continues with the spear-thrust producing blood and water (19:34), pre-empting docetic readings of the death

peinaō G3983 = to hunger. Aorist indicative active. After fasting forty days and forty nights, Jesus hungered. The narrative grounds his real human bodily need for food, the point of vulnerability the tempter exploits in the wilderness

scripture.key_term: Ἰησοῦν Χριστὸν ἐν σαρκὶ ἐληλυθότα (Iēsoun Christon en sarki elēlythota) G4561 + G2064
scripture.force: elēlythota = having come (perfect active participle of erchomai G2064, completed action with continuing state). 'Confess that Jesus Christ has come in the flesh.' The perfect tense asserts the incarnation as a completed event whose effects persist; Christ remains in the flesh, not merely came in the flesh for a time. Confession of this is the test of whether a spirit is from God

scripture.key_term: πᾶν πνεῦμα ὃ μὴ ὁμολογεῖ τὸν Ἰησοῦν... ἀντιχρίστου (antichristou) G500
scripture.force: antichristos G500 = antichrist. The denial of Christ come in the flesh is named as the spirit of antichrist. Scripture itself supplies the cult-marker verdict in its own lexical pattern: denial of Christ's true incarnation is not a Christological dispute, it is the antichrist spirit. Unique among the questions in the baseline for this canonical naming

scripture.key_term: οἱ μὴ ὁμολογοῦντες Ἰησοῦν Χριστὸν ἐρχόμενον ἐν σαρκί
scripture.force: Many deceivers have gone out into the world, those who do not confess Iēsoun Christon erchomenon en sarki (Jesus Christ coming in flesh). 'This is the deceiver and the antichrist.' Parallel to 1 John 4:2-3, reinforces the lexical naming of denial as antichrist. Present middle participle erchomenon underscores the ongoing reality of his incarnate state

scripture.key_term: ἐν ὁμοιώματι σαρκὸς ἁμαρτίας (en homoiōmati sarkos hamartias) G3667 + G4561
scripture.force: 'In the likeness of sinful flesh.' homoiōma G3667 here is the same noun used in Phil 2:7. Paul carefully phrases Christ as in the likeness of sinful flesh, not in sinful flesh, to preserve sinlessness while affirming full humanity. The phrase complicates a naive reading (does 'likeness' mean not really flesh?) but the parallel with Heb 4:15 chōris hamartias resolves it: full humanity, no participation in sin

scripture.key_term: ἐφανερώθη ἐν σαρκί (ephanerōthē en sarki) G5319 + G4561
scripture.force: phaneroō G5319 = to make visible, to manifest. Aorist passive indicative. 'He was manifested in flesh.' Paul's hymnic confession of the mystery of godliness opens with the incarnation. The verb phaneroō with en sarki echoes John 1:14 conceptually: real visible manifestation in a real body, not appearance in the docetic sense

scripture.key_term: δεήσεις τε καὶ ἱκετηρίας... μετὰ κραυγῆς ἰσχυρᾶς καὶ δακρύων (kraugēs ischyras kai dakryōn) G2906 + G2403 + G1144
scripture.force: 'In the days of his flesh, having offered up both prayers and supplications with loud cries and tears.' kraugē G2906 = loud cry, dakry G1144 = tear. The epistle to the Hebrews names 'the days of his flesh' (sarx G4561) as the time when Jesus prayed with loud cries and tears. Real human emotional and physical anguish, presumably in Gethsemane and at the cross

scripture.key_term: πάτερ εἰς χεῖράς σου παρατίθεμαι τὸ πνεῦμά μου (pneuma) G4151
scripture.force: pneuma G4151 = spirit. At death Jesus commits his spirit to the Father. With Luke 2:52 (advancing in wisdom) and John 12:27 (psychē tetaraktai, soul troubled) and Matt 26:38 (perilypos hē psychē mou, soul sorrowful unto death), the Gospels supply the lexical basis for the Chalcedonian 'rational soul' clause: Jesus has psychē, pneuma, and the developmental capacity of a real human mind

hermeneutics.notes: Verdict rests on a pan-canonical lexical network across all four Gospels, Acts, the Pauline corpus, Hebrews, and the Johannine epistles. The key lemmas are sarx G4561 (149 NT occurrences, repeatedly applied to Christ), anthrōpos G444 (over 550 NT occurrences, applied to Christ in 1 Tim 2:5, John 8:40, John 19:5, Acts 2:22, Acts 17:31, 1 Cor 15:21, 1 Cor 15:47, Rom 5:15), haima G129 (Christ's blood as real human blood in Heb 2:14, Heb 9:12-14, 1 Pet 1:19, Rev 1:5), and the participial constructions of erchomai G2064 perfect (1 John 4:2, 2 John 7) and ginomai G1096 aorist (John 1:14, Phil 2:7, Rom 1:3). The proposition is settled at the lexical level, requires no exotic framework, and is confessed identically by every framework in play: covenant, dispensational, new-covenant, progressive-covenantalism, redemptive-historical, and patristic-typological all confess Christ's full humanity. Analogia scripturae is invoked at the resolution of the Rom 8:3 'likeness of sinful flesh' complication: Heb 4:15 chōris hamartias and 2 Cor 5:21 (he who knew no sin) resolve the apparent tension by distinguishing full humanity from participation in sin. Progressive revelation is not the operative factor here; the New Testament writers explicitly anchor the incarnation in Old Testament prophecy (Isa 7:14 Immanuel, Isa 53 the suffering servant, Mic 5:2 Bethlehem, Ps 22 the crucifixion psalm), making this a New Testament reality with explicit Old Testament typological pre-figuring rather than a doctrine that develops in revelation.

counter_witness.anchor: Ignatius of Antioch, Letter to the Trallians 9 (c. 110 AD)
counter_witness.key_phrase: He was truly born, and ate and drank. He was truly persecuted under Pontius Pilate; He was truly crucified, and died. Direct anti-docetic confession from the earliest post-apostolic generation

counter_witness.anchor: Ignatius of Antioch, Letter to the Smyrnaeans 1-2 (c. 110 AD)
counter_witness.key_phrase: He was truly of the seed of David according to the flesh, and was truly, under Pontius Pilate and Herod the tetrarch, nailed for us in His flesh. Refutes those who say he only seemed to suffer

Truly God and truly man, of a reasonable soul and body; consubstantial with us according to the Manhood; in all things like unto us, without sin. The ecumenical creedal floor for all subsequent Christology

CCC 470: Christ assumed a true human body and a rational soul. The Son of God worked with human hands, thought with a human mind, acted with a human will, loved with a human heart. Direct Chalcedonian confession

counter_witness.anchor: Augsburg Confession Article III, Of the Son of God (1530)
counter_witness.key_phrase: The Word, that is, the Son of God, did assume the human nature in the womb of the blessed Virgin Mary, so that there are two natures, the divine and the human, inseparably enjoined in one Person, true God and true man

counter_witness.anchor: 39 Articles, Article II, Of the Word or Son of God, which was made very Man
counter_witness.key_phrase: Took man's nature in the womb of the blessed Virgin, of her substance: so that two whole and perfect Natures, the Godhead and Manhood, were joined together in one Person, never to be divided

counter_witness.anchor: Westminster Confession 8.2, Of Christ the Mediator
counter_witness.key_phrase: The Son of God did take upon Him man's nature, with all the essential properties and common infirmities thereof, yet without sin; two whole, perfect, and distinct natures inseparably joined together in one person

counter_witness.anchor: Methodist Articles of Religion, Article II (1784, abridged from 39 Articles)
counter_witness.key_phrase: The Son took man's nature in the womb of the blessed Virgin; so that two whole and perfect natures, the Godhead and Manhood, were joined together in one person, never to be divided

counter_witness.anchor: Dordrecht Confession Article 4, Of the Coming of Christ Into This World (1632)
counter_witness.key_phrase: We confess and believe that this same Son was conceived of the Holy Ghost in the Virgin Mary, and that He was born of her, and thus became flesh and man, and was manifested in the flesh

counter_witness.anchor: Assemblies of God Statement of Fundamental Truths, The Deity of the Lord Jesus Christ
counter_witness.key_phrase: The Lord Jesus Christ is the eternal Son of God. The Scriptures declare His virgin birth, His sinless life, His miracles, His vicarious and atoning death, His bodily resurrection, His exaltation

counter_witness.anchor: Nicene-Constantinopolitan Creed (381) as used in Eastern Orthodox liturgy
counter_witness.key_phrase: Was incarnate of the Holy Spirit and the Virgin Mary and became man (enanthrōpēsanta). And was crucified for us under Pontius Pilate, and suffered, and was buried. Confessed weekly in Orthodox liturgy

web.quote: John 1:14 ho logos sarx egeneto: sarx G4561 (flesh) became (egeneto G1096 aorist indicative) the predicate of the eternal Logos. Predicate nominative construction asserts essential transformation
1 Tim 2:5: anthrōpos Christos Iēsous, the one mediator between God and humanity. anthrōpos G444 functions as appositional noun naming the human nature of the mediator

Heb 2:14 paraplēsiōs meteschen tōn autōn (G3898 + G3348): he likewise shared in the same things. The adverb 'similarly' with aorist of metechō establishes parallel participation in flesh and blood with the children
Heb 2:17 kata panta tois adelphois homoiōthēnai (G3666 aorist passive infinitive): he had to be made like his brothers in every respect. Comprehensive identification with humanity
Heb 4:15 pepeirasmenon kata panta kath' homoiotēta chōris hamartias (G3985 + G3665 + G266): tested in every way according to our likeness, yet without sin. Two clauses, full humanity and no sin

1 John 4:2 Iēsoun Christon en sarki elēlythota (G2064 perfect active participle): Jesus Christ has come in the flesh. Perfect tense asserts completed event with continuing state, the test of a spirit being from God
Phil 2:7 en homoiōmati anthrōpōn genomenos (G3667 + G1096): being made in the likeness of men. Followed by schēmati heuretheis hōs anthrōpos in v.8 closing any docetic loophole
Luke 2:52 proekopten sophia kai hēlikia (G4298 imperfect): he kept advancing in wisdom and stature. Imperfect tense rules out pretended growth; real human mental and physical development
sarx G4561, 149 NT occurrences. Pan-canonical Christological uses: John 1:14, Rom 1:3, Rom 8:3, 1 Tim 3:16, Heb 2:14, Heb 5:7, Heb 10:20, 1 Pet 3:18, 1 Pet 4:1, 1 John 4:2-3, 2 John 7
anthrōpos G444, 554 NT occurrences. Applied to Christ in 1 Tim 2:5, John 8:40, John 19:5 (ecce homo), Acts 2:22, Acts 17:31, 1 Cor 15:21, 1 Cor 15:47 (the second man from heaven), Rom 5:15
Ignatius to the Trallians 9: He was truly born, and ate and drank. He was truly persecuted under Pontius Pilate; He was truly crucified, and died. Early second-century refutation of docetism
Ignatius to the Smyrnaeans 1-2: truly of the seed of David according to the flesh, truly nailed in his flesh under Pontius Pilate. Direct anti-docetic confession

Augsburg III: the Word, the Son of God, did assume human nature in the womb of the blessed Virgin Mary, so that there are two natures, divine and human, inseparably enjoined in one Person, true God and true man

Methodist Articles II: The Son took man's nature in the womb of the blessed Virgin; so that two whole and perfect natures, the Godhead and Manhood, were joined together in one person, never to be divided

Chalcedonian Definition (AD 451): Truly God and truly man, of a reasonable soul and body; consubstantial with us according to the Manhood; in all things like unto us, without sin. The ecumenical Christological floor
